title: Capstone Projects
description: Four practical, full-stack Android projects: Weather Dashboard, Offline-First Notes, Real-Time Chat, and Multi-Module Task Manager
lesson:
Capstone Projects Overview

These four projects progressively build your skills from networking and state management through offline-first architecture, real-time communication, and multi-module app design. Each project is a complete, runnable app with Jetpack Compose, ViewModel, and Hilt.

How to use these projects:
1. Read the architecture diagram and understand the data flow
2. Create the project in Android Studio and add dependencies
3. Copy the boilerplate code as your starting point
4. Follow the implementation guidance for the hard parts
5. Run, test, and extend with your own features

Project 1: Weather Dashboard App

What You Will Build

A weather app that shows current conditions and a 5-day forecast for any city. Users can search for locations, pull to refresh data, and see loading/error states handled gracefully. This project teaches networking with Retrofit, state management with ViewModel + StateFlow, error handling, and Compose layouts.

Skills practiced: Retrofit + kotlinx.serialization, Hilt DI, ViewModel, StateFlow, Compose layouts (LazyColumn, Card, Row/Column), pull-to-refresh, error handling with sealed classes

Android Studio Setup

Step 1: File > New > New Project > Empty Activity (Compose). Name: WeatherDashboard. Package: com.example.weatherdashboard. Min SDK: API 26. Build config language: Kotlin DSL.

Step 2: Open build.gradle.kts (Module :app) and add these dependencies:

// build.gradle.kts (Module :app) plugins { alias(libs.plugins.android.application) alias(libs.plugins.kotlin.android) alias(libs.plugins.kotlin.compose) id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android") id("com.google.devtools.ksp") kotlin("plugin.serialization") } dependencies { // Compose BOM (already in template) implementation(platform("androidx.compose:compose-bom:2024.12.01")) implementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui") implementation("androidx.compose.material3:material3") implementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-tooling-preview") debugImplementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-tooling") // ViewModel + Compose integration implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-viewmodel-compose:2.8.7") implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-compose:2.8.7") // Hilt implementation("com.google.dagger:hilt-android:2.52") ksp("com.google.dagger:hilt-compiler:2.52") implementation("androidx.hilt:hilt-navigation-compose:1.2.0") // Retrofit + kotlinx.serialization implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:retrofit:2.11.0") implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-serialization-json:1.7.3") implementation("com.jakewharton.retrofit:retrofit2-kotlinx-serialization-converter:1.0.0") implementation("com.squareup.okhttp3:okhttp:4.12.0") implementation("com.squareup.okhttp3:logging-interceptor:4.12.0") // Coil for weather icons implementation("io.coil-kt:coil-compose:2.7.0") }

Step 3: In build.gradle.kts (Project-level), add the Hilt and serialization plugins:

// build.gradle.kts (Project-level) — plugins block plugins { id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android") version "2.52" apply false id("com.google.devtools.ksp") version "2.0.21-1.0.27" apply false kotlin("plugin.serialization") version "2.0.21" apply false }

Step 4: Get a free API key from openweathermap.org/api. Sign up, go to API Keys, copy your key.

Data Classes

// data/model/WeatherModels.kt package com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model import kotlinx.serialization.SerialName import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable @Serializable data class CurrentWeatherResponse( val name: String, val main: MainData, val weather: List<WeatherDescription>, val wind: WindData, val sys: SysData ) @Serializable data class MainData( val temp: Double, @SerialName("feels_like") val feelsLike: Double, val humidity: Int, @SerialName("temp_min") val tempMin: Double, @SerialName("temp_max") val tempMax: Double ) @Serializable data class WeatherDescription( val id: Int, val main: String, val description: String, val icon: String ) @Serializable data class WindData( val speed: Double ) @Serializable data class SysData( val country: String ) @Serializable data class ForecastResponse( val list: List<ForecastItem> ) @Serializable data class ForecastItem( val dt: Long, val main: MainData, val weather: List<WeatherDescription>, @SerialName("dt_txt") val dtTxt: String ) // Domain models (UI-friendly) data class WeatherData( val cityName: String, val country: String, val tempCelsius: Double, val feelsLikeCelsius: Double, val description: String, val iconUrl: String, val humidity: Int, val windSpeed: Double, val tempMin: Double, val tempMax: Double ) data class ForecastDay( val dateText: String, val tempCelsius: Double, val tempMin: Double, val tempMax: Double, val description: String, val iconUrl: String )

Retrofit API Interface

// data/remote/WeatherApi.kt package com.example.weatherdashboard.data.remote import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.CurrentWeatherResponse import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.ForecastResponse import retrofit2.http.GET import retrofit2.http.Query interface WeatherApi { @GET("weather") suspend fun getCurrentWeather( @Query("q") city: String, @Query("appid") apiKey: String, @Query("units") units: String = "metric" ): CurrentWeatherResponse @GET("forecast") suspend fun getForecast( @Query("q") city: String, @Query("appid") apiKey: String, @Query("units") units: String = "metric" ): ForecastResponse companion object { const val BASE_URL = "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/" } }

Hilt Module

// di/AppModule.kt package com.example.weatherdashboard.di import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.remote.WeatherApi import com.jakewharton.retrofit2.converter.kotlinx.serialization.asConverterFactory import dagger.Module import dagger.Provides import dagger.hilt.InstallIn import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent import kotlinx.serialization.json.Json import okhttp3.MediaType.Companion.toMediaType import okhttp3.OkHttpClient import okhttp3.logging.HttpLoggingInterceptor import retrofit2.Retrofit import javax.inject.Singleton @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object AppModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideJson(): Json = Json { ignoreUnknownKeys = true coerceInputValues = true } @Provides @Singleton fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor( HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply { level = HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY } ) .build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(client: OkHttpClient, json: Json): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(WeatherApi.BASE_URL) .client(client) .addConverterFactory(json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideWeatherApi(retrofit: Retrofit): WeatherApi = retrofit.create(WeatherApi::class.java) }

Repository

// data/repository/WeatherRepository.kt package com.example.weatherdashboard.data.repository import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.ForecastDay import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.WeatherData import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.remote.WeatherApi import javax.inject.Inject import javax.inject.Singleton @Singleton class WeatherRepository @Inject constructor( private val api: WeatherApi ) { // Replace with your actual API key private val apiKey = "YOUR_API_KEY_HERE" suspend fun getCurrentWeather(city: String): WeatherData { val response = api.getCurrentWeather(city, apiKey) val weather = response.weather.first() return WeatherData( cityName = response.name, country = response.sys.country, tempCelsius = response.main.temp, feelsLikeCelsius = response.main.feelsLike, description = weather.description.replaceFirstChar { it.uppercase() }, iconUrl = "https://openweathermap.org/img/wn/${weather.icon}@2x.png", humidity = response.main.humidity, windSpeed = response.wind.speed, tempMin = response.main.tempMin, tempMax = response.main.tempMax ) } suspend fun getForecast(city: String): List<ForecastDay> { val response = api.getForecast(city, apiKey) // API returns 3-hour intervals; take one entry per day (noon) return response.list .filter { it.dtTxt.contains("12:00:00") } .map { item -> val weather = item.weather.first() ForecastDay( dateText = item.dtTxt.substringBefore(" "), tempCelsius = item.main.temp, tempMin = item.main.tempMin, tempMax = item.main.tempMax, description = weather.description.replaceFirstChar { it.uppercase() }, iconUrl = "https://openweathermap.org/img/wn/${weather.icon}@2x.png" ) } } }

ViewModel

// ui/WeatherViewModel.kt package com.example.weatherdashboard.ui import androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel import androidx.lifecycle.viewModelScope import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.ForecastDay import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.model.WeatherData import com.example.weatherdashboard.data.repository.WeatherRepository import dagger.hilt.android.lifecycle.HiltViewModel import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableStateFlow import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.StateFlow import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asStateFlow import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.update import kotlinx.coroutines.launch import javax.inject.Inject sealed interface WeatherUiState { data object Initial : WeatherUiState data object Loading : WeatherUiState data class Success( val weather: WeatherData, val forecast: List<ForecastDay>, val isRefreshing: Boolean = false ) : WeatherUiState data class Error(val message: String) : WeatherUiState } @HiltViewModel class WeatherViewModel @Inject constructor( private val repository: WeatherRepository ) : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<WeatherUiState>(WeatherUiState.Initial) val uiState: StateFlow<WeatherUiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() private var currentCity: String = "" fun search(city: String) { if (city.isBlank()) return currentCity = city.trim() _uiState.value = WeatherUiState.Loading loadWeather() } fun refresh() { if (currentCity.isBlank()) return val current = _uiState.value if (current is WeatherUiState.Success) { _uiState.update { current.copy(isRefreshing = true) } } loadWeather() } private fun loadWeather() { viewModelScope.launch { try { val weather = repository.getCurrentWeather(currentCity) val forecast = repository.getForecast(currentCity) _uiState.value = WeatherUiState.Success( weather = weather, forecast = forecast ) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = WeatherUiState.Error( message = when { e.message?.contains("404") == true -> "City not found. Check the spelling." e.message?.contains("401") == true -> "Invalid API key. Check your configuration." else -> "Network error: ${e.localizedMessage}" } ) } } } }

How to Run and Test

Running:
1. Replace YOUR_API_KEY_HERE in WeatherRepository with your OpenWeatherMap key
2. Add INTERNET permission to AndroidManifest.xml
3. Add android:name=".WeatherApp" to the application tag
4. Run on emulator or device
5. Type a city name (e.g., "London") and press search

Testing: Create a fake WeatherRepository and inject it via Hilt's testing module to test the ViewModel without network calls. Verify each UiState transition.

Project 2: Offline-First Notes App

What You Will Build

A notes app that works entirely offline using Room as the single source of truth. Notes sync to a remote server via WorkManager when connectivity is available. The app shows a sync status indicator and handles conflicts. This project teaches Room database, offline-first architecture, WorkManager, and Flow-based reactive data.

Skills practiced: Room (Entity, DAO, Database), WorkManager for background sync, Flow for reactive UI, offline-first pattern (local-first writes), conflict detection, Compose Navigation

Android Studio Setup

Step 1: File > New > New Project > Empty Activity (Compose). Name: OfflineNotes. Package: com.example.offlinenotes. Min SDK: API 26.

Step 2: Dependencies in build.gradle.kts (Module :app):

Architecture Diagram

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ UI Layer │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐ │ │ │ NoteListScreen│ ─────▶│ NoteEditScreen │ │ │ │ (LazyColumn) │ ◀─────│ (title + body) │ │ │ └──────┬───────┘ └───────┬───────────┘ │ └─────────┼────────────────────────┼─────────────────┘ │ collectAsState │ save/delete ▼ ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ NoteViewModel │ │ notes: StateFlow<List<NoteEntity>> │ │ syncStatus: StateFlow<SyncStatus> │ └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ NoteRepository │ │ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Local: Room DAO │ │ SyncScheduler │ │ │ │ (source of truth)│ │ (WorkManager wrapper)│ │ │ └────────┬────────┘ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ reads/writes schedules sync │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ NoteDatabase │ │ SyncWorker │ │ │ │ (Room SQLite) │ │ (WorkManager) │ │ │ └─────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Room Entity and DAO

// data/local/NoteEntity.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.local import androidx.room.Entity import androidx.room.PrimaryKey enum class SyncState { SYNCED, // Matches server PENDING, // Local change not yet pushed CONFLICT // Server has a different version } @Entity(tableName = "notes") data class NoteEntity( @PrimaryKey val id: String = java.util.UUID.randomUUID().toString(), val title: String, val body: String, val createdAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val updatedAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val syncState: SyncState = SyncState.PENDING, val isDeleted: Boolean = false // Soft delete for sync )
// data/local/NoteDao.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.local import androidx.room.* import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow @Dao interface NoteDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE isDeleted = 0 ORDER BY updatedAt DESC") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE id = :id") suspend fun getById(id: String): NoteEntity? @Upsert suspend fun upsert(note: NoteEntity) @Query("UPDATE notes SET isDeleted = 1, syncState = 'PENDING', updatedAt = :now WHERE id = :id") suspend fun softDelete(id: String, now: Long = System.currentTimeMillis()) @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncState = 'PENDING'") suspend fun getPendingSyncNotes(): List<NoteEntity> @Query("UPDATE notes SET syncState = 'SYNCED' WHERE id = :id") suspend fun markSynced(id: String) @Query("DELETE FROM notes WHERE id = :id AND isDeleted = 1 AND syncState = 'SYNCED'") suspend fun permanentlyDelete(id: String) @Query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM notes WHERE syncState = 'PENDING'") fun observePendingCount(): Flow<Int> }

Room Database

// data/local/NoteDatabase.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.local import androidx.room.Database import androidx.room.RoomDatabase @Database( entities = [NoteEntity::class], version = 1, exportSchema = false ) abstract class NoteDatabase : RoomDatabase() { abstract fun noteDao(): NoteDao }

Sync Scheduler (WorkManager Wrapper)

// data/sync/SyncScheduler.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.sync import androidx.work.* import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit import javax.inject.Inject import javax.inject.Singleton @Singleton class SyncScheduler @Inject constructor( private val workManager: WorkManager ) { companion object { private const val SYNC_WORK_NAME = "note_sync" } // One-time sync when user makes a change fun scheduleImmediateSync() { val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>() .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .setBackoffCriteria( BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, 30, TimeUnit.SECONDS ) .build() workManager.enqueueUniqueWork( SYNC_WORK_NAME, ExistingWorkPolicy.REPLACE, request ) } // Periodic sync every 15 minutes fun schedulePeriodicSync() { val request = PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>( repeatInterval = 15, TimeUnit.MINUTES ) .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .build() workManager.enqueueUniquePeriodicWork( "${SYNC_WORK_NAME}_periodic", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP, request ) } }

SyncWorker

// data/sync/SyncWorker.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.sync import android.content.Context import androidx.hilt.work.HiltWorker import androidx.work.CoroutineWorker import androidx.work.WorkerParameters import com.example.offlinenotes.data.local.NoteDao import dagger.assisted.Assisted import dagger.assisted.AssistedInject @HiltWorker class SyncWorker @AssistedInject constructor( @Assisted context: Context, @Assisted params: WorkerParameters, private val noteDao: NoteDao ) : CoroutineWorker(context, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { return try { val pendingNotes = noteDao.getPendingSyncNotes() for (note in pendingNotes) { // TODO: Replace with actual API call // val serverResponse = api.syncNote(note) // For now, simulate successful sync kotlinx.coroutines.delay(100) // simulate network noteDao.markSynced(note.id) // If the note was soft-deleted and now synced, remove it if (note.isDeleted) { noteDao.permanentlyDelete(note.id) } } Result.success() } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 3) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } }

Repository

// data/repository/NoteRepository.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.data.repository import com.example.offlinenotes.data.local.NoteDao import com.example.offlinenotes.data.local.NoteEntity import com.example.offlinenotes.data.local.SyncState import com.example.offlinenotes.data.sync.SyncScheduler import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow import javax.inject.Inject import javax.inject.Singleton @Singleton class NoteRepository @Inject constructor( private val noteDao: NoteDao, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler ) { fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> = noteDao.observeAll() fun observePendingSyncCount(): Flow<Int> = noteDao.observePendingCount() suspend fun getNote(id: String): NoteEntity? = noteDao.getById(id) suspend fun saveNote(title: String, body: String, existingId: String? = null) { val note = if (existingId != null) { val existing = noteDao.getById(existingId) existing?.copy( title = title, body = body, updatedAt = System.currentTimeMillis(), syncState = SyncState.PENDING ) ?: NoteEntity(title = title, body = body) } else { NoteEntity(title = title, body = body) } noteDao.upsert(note) syncScheduler.scheduleImmediateSync() } suspend fun deleteNote(id: String) { noteDao.softDelete(id) syncScheduler.scheduleImmediateSync() } }

// ui/NoteViewModel.kt package com.example.offlinenotes.ui import androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel import androidx.lifecycle.viewModelScope import com.example.offlinenotes.data.local.NoteEntity import com.example.offlinenotes.data.repository.NoteRepository import dagger.hilt.android.lifecycle.HiltViewModel import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.* import kotlinx.coroutines.launch import javax.inject.Inject data class NoteListUiState( val notes: List<NoteEntity> = emptyList(), val pendingSyncCount: Int = 0 ) data class NoteEditUiState( val id: String? How to Run and Test

Running:
1. Set up Hilt Application class and @AndroidEntryPoint on MainActivity
2. Set up Navigation: NoteListScreen -> NoteEditScreen(noteId?)

Project 3: Real-Time Chat App

What You Will Build

A chat app with real-time messaging using Firebase Realtime Database (or WebSocket). Messages appear instantly, there is a typing indicator, online status tracking, and message history is cached in Room. This project teaches real-time data synchronization, service communication, message caching, and performant LazyColumn usage.

Skills practiced: Firebase Realtime Database listeners, real-time state management, Room as message cache, LazyColumn with stable keys, typing indicator with debounce, Compose chat bubble UI

Step 1: File > New > New Project > Empty Activity (Compose). Name: RealtimeChat. Package: com.example.realtimechat. Min SDK: API 26.

Step 2: Connect to Firebase: Tools > Firebase > Realtime Database > Connect. This adds google-services.json to your project.

Data Classes

// data/model/Message.kt package com.example.realtimechat.data.model import androidx.room.Entity import androidx.room.PrimaryKey @Entity(tableName = "messages") data class Message( @PrimaryKey val id: String = "", val chatRoomId: String = "", val senderId: String = "", val senderName: String = "", val text: String = "", val timestamp: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val isRead: Boolean = false ) { // No-arg constructor required by Firebase constructor() : this(id = "") } data class ChatUser( val uid: String = "", val displayName: String = "", val isOnline: Boolean = false, val lastSeen: Long = 0 ) data class TypingStatus( val userId: String = "", val isTyping: Boolean = false, val timestamp: Long = 0 )

Room Cache DAO

// data/local/MessageDao.kt package com.example.realtimechat.data.local import androidx.room.* import com.example.realtimechat.data.model.Message import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow @Dao interface MessageDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM messages WHERE chatRoomId = :roomId ORDER BY timestamp ASC") fun observeMessages(roomId: String): Flow<List<Message>> @Upsert suspend fun upsertAll(messages: List<Message>) @Upsert suspend fun upsert(message: Message) @Query("DELETE FROM messages WHERE chatRoomId = :roomId") suspend fun clearRoom(roomId: String) } @Database( entities = [Message::class], version = 1, exportSchema = false ) abstract class ChatDatabase : RoomDatabase() { abstract fun messageDao(): MessageDao }

Repository with Firebase + Room

// data/repository/ChatRepository.kt package com.example.realtimechat.data.repository import com.example.realtimechat.data.local.MessageDao import com.example.realtimechat.data.model.ChatUser import com.example.realtimechat.data.model.Message import com.example.realtimechat.data.model.TypingStatus import com.google.firebase.auth.FirebaseAuth import com.google.firebase.database.* import kotlinx.coroutines.channels.awaitClose import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.callbackFlow import kotlinx.coroutines.tasks.await import javax.inject.Inject import javax.inject.Singleton @Singleton class ChatRepository @Inject constructor( private val firebaseDb: FirebaseDatabase, private val auth: FirebaseAuth, private val messageDao: MessageDao ) { private val messagesRef = firebaseDb.getReference("messages") private val typingRef = firebaseDb.getReference("typing") private val usersRef = firebaseDb.getReference("users") val currentUserId: String get() = auth.currentUser?.uid ?: "anonymous" val currentUserName: String get() = auth.currentUser?.displayName ?: "Anonymous" // Observe messages in real-time from Firebase, // caching them in Room as they arrive fun observeMessages(roomId: String): Flow<List<Message>> = callbackFlow { val listener = messagesRef.child(roomId) .orderByChild("timestamp") .addValueEventListener(object : ValueEventListener { override fun onDataChange(snapshot: DataSnapshot) { val messages = snapshot.children.mapNotNull { it.getValue(Message::class.java)?.copy(id = it.key ?: "") } // Cache in Room for offline access kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking { messageDao.upsertAll(messages) } trySend(messages) } override fun onCancelled(error: DatabaseError) { close(error.toException()) } }) awaitClose { messagesRef.child(roomId).removeEventListener(listener) } } // Fallback: cached messages when offline fun observeCachedMessages(roomId: String): Flow<List<Message>> = messageDao.observeMessages(roomId) suspend fun sendMessage(roomId: String, text: String) { val key = messagesRef.child(roomId).push().key ?: return val message = Message( id = key, chatRoomId = roomId, senderId = currentUserId, senderName = currentUserName, text = text, timestamp = ServerValue.TIMESTAMP.hashCode().toLong() ) // Write to Firebase — it syncs to all listeners messagesRef.child(roomId).child(key).setValue(message).await() } fun setTypingStatus(roomId: String, isTyping: Boolean) { val status = TypingStatus( userId = currentUserId, isTyping = isTyping, timestamp = System.currentTimeMillis() ) typingRef.child(roomId).child(currentUserId).setValue(status) } fun observeTypingStatus(roomId: String): Flow<Boolean> = callbackFlow { val listener = typingRef.child(roomId) .addValueEventListener(object : ValueEventListener { override fun onDataChange(snapshot: DataSnapshot) { val isOtherTyping = snapshot.children.any { child -> val status = child.getValue(TypingStatus::class.java) status != null && status.userId != currentUserId && status.isTyping && System.currentTimeMillis() - status.timestamp < 5000 } trySend(isOtherTyping) } override fun onCancelled(error: DatabaseError) { close(error.toException()) } }) awaitClose { typingRef.child(roomId).removeEventListener(listener) } } fun setOnlineStatus(isOnline: Boolean) { val userRef = usersRef.child(currentUserId) userRef.child("isOnline").setValue(isOnline) if (!isOnline) { userRef.child("lastSeen").setValue(ServerValue.TIMESTAMP) } // Auto-set offline when connection drops userRef.child("isOnline").onDisconnect().setValue(false) userRef.child("lastSeen").onDisconnect().setValue(ServerValue.TIMESTAMP) } }

ViewModel

// ui/ChatViewModel.kt package com.example.realtimechat.ui import androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel import androidx.lifecycle.viewModelScope import com.example.realtimechat.data.model.Message import com.example.realtimechat.data.repository.ChatRepository import dagger.hilt.android.lifecycle.HiltViewModel import kotlinx.coroutines.Job import kotlinx.coroutines.delay import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.* import kotlinx.coroutines.launch import javax.inject.Inject @HiltViewModel class ChatViewModel @Inject constructor( private val repository: ChatRepository ) : ViewModel() { private val roomId = "general" // default room val messages: StateFlow<List<Message>> = repository.observeMessages(roomId) .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = emptyList() ) val isOtherTyping: StateFlow<Boolean> = repository.observeTypingStatus(roomId) .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = false ) val currentUserId: String get() = repository.currentUserId private var typingJob: Job? = null init { repository.setOnlineStatus(true) } fun sendMessage(text: String) { if (text.isBlank()) return viewModelScope.launch { repository.sendMessage(roomId, text.trim()) repository.setTypingStatus(roomId, false) } } fun onTextChanged(text: String) { // Debounced typing indicator: // Set typing=true immediately, then false after 2s of inactivity repository.setTypingStatus(roomId, text.isNotEmpty()) typingJob?.cancel() typingJob = viewModelScope.launch { delay(2_000) repository.setTypingStatus(roomId, false) } } override fun onCleared() { super.onCleared() repository.setOnlineStatus(false) repository.setTypingStatus(roomId, false) } }

} ) { message -> MessageBubble( message = message, isOwnMessage = message.senderId == viewModel.currentUserId ) } } // Typing indicator if (isOtherTyping) { Text( text = "Someone is typing...", style MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurfaceVariant val alignment = if (isOwnMessage) Alignment.End else Alignment.Start val shape = RoundedCornerShape( topStart = 16.dp, topEnd = 16.dp, bottomStart = if How to Run and Test

Running:
1. Set up Firebase project at console.firebase.google.com
2. Add google-services.json to the app/ directory
3. Enable Anonymous Auth in Firebase Console > Authentication
4. Set Realtime Database rules to allow read/write (for testing)
5. Sign in anonymously on app launch (add to MainActivity/ViewModel init)
6. Run on two emulators/devices to test real-time messaging

Key implementation guidance:
- Use callbackFlow to bridge Firebase callbacks to Kotlin Flow
- Use reverseLayout = true on LazyColumn for chat (newest at bottom)
- Always provide stable key in LazyColumn items for performance
- Debounce typing indicator to avoid excessive Firebase writes

Project 4: Task Manager with Multi-Module Architecture

What You Will Build

A full-featured task manager with categories, due dates, priority sorting, and search. The distinguishing feature is its multi-module architecture: the app is split into :app, :core:data, :core:ui, :feature:tasklist, and :feature:taskdetail. // core/data/src/.../repository/TaskRepository.kt package com.example.taskmanager.core.data.repository import com.example.taskmanager.core.data.local.TaskDao import com.example.taskmanager.core.data.model.Priority import com.example.taskmanager.core.data.model.TaskCategory import com.example.taskmanager.core.data.model.TaskEntity import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.Flow import javax.inject.Inject import javax.inject.Singleton @Singleton class TaskRepository @Inject constructor( private val taskDao: TaskDao ) { fun observeAllTasks(): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> = taskDao.observeAll() fun observeTask(id: Long): Flow<TaskEntity?> = taskDao.observeById(id) fun observeByCategory(category: TaskCategory): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> = taskDao.observeByCategory(category) fun searchTasks(query: String): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> = taskDao.search(query) suspend fun getTask(id: Long): TaskEntity? = taskDao.getById(id) suspend fun saveTask( title: String, description: String = "", priority: Priority = Priority.MEDIUM, category: TaskCategory = TaskCategory.OTHER, dueDate: Long? = null, existingId: Long? = null ) { val task = if (existingId != null && existingId != 0L) { val existing = taskDao.getById(existingId) existing?.copy( title = title, description = description, priority = priority, category = category, dueDate = dueDate, updatedAt = System.currentTimeMillis() ) ?: TaskEntity( title = title, description = description, priority = priority, category = category, dueDate = dueDate ) } else { TaskEntity( title = title, description = description, priority = priority, category = category, dueDate = dueDate ) } taskDao.upsert(task) } suspend fun toggleComplete(id: Long, completed: Boolean) { taskDao.setCompleted(id, completed) } suspend fun deleteTask(task: TaskEntity) { taskDao.delete(task) } }

How to Run and Test

Running:
1. Create all 5 modules in Android Studio (app + 4 library modules)
2. Set up the module dependency graph in each build.gradle.kts
3. Ensure Hilt Application class is in :app
4. Run on device; create tasks with different priorities and categories
5. Test search and category filtering

Testing each layer independently:
- :core:data — Test DAO with Room in-memory DB, test Repository with fake DAO
- :core:ui — Preview composables, screenshot tests
- :feature:tasklist — Test ViewModel with fake Repository
- :feature:taskdetail — Test ViewModel with SavedStateHandle
- :app — Integration tests with full navigation
Multi-module gotchas:
- Room annotations (@Entity, @Dao) must be in the SAME module as @Database, or use Room's multi-module support with @AutoMigration
- Hilt modules in library modules work automatically — Hilt discovers @Module classes at compile time
- Each module that uses KSP (Room, Hilt) must apply the KSP plugin independently
- Feature modules should NEVER depend on each other — communicate only via :core or navigation

questions.title: Repository Pattern Purpose
questions.description: In the Weather Dashboard, why does the WeatherRepository exist between the ViewModel and the WeatherApi Retrofit interface?
options: It is required by Hilt for dependency injection to work

It abstracts the data source — the ViewModel does not know whether data comes from network, cache, or a fake. This enables testing and future changes (e.g., adding a cache) without touching the ViewModel.
It converts Retrofit calls from asynchronous to synchronous
It is a Google-required pattern for publishing apps on the Play Store
questions.explanation: The Repository pattern provides a clean API to the ViewModel. The ViewModel calls repository.getCurrentWeather(city) without knowing if it hits the network, reads from cache, or returns test data. This separation lets you swap in a FakeWeatherRepository during tests, and later add a caching layer without changing ViewModel code. It is a recommendation from Android architecture guidelines, not a technical requirement.
hints: Think about what the ViewModel 'knows' about where data comes from
Consider how you would test the ViewModel without real network calls

questions.title: Offline-First Write Strategy
questions.description: In the Notes app, when the user saves a note while offline, what happens?
options: The save fails and the user sees an error
The note is queued in memory and written when connectivity returns
The note is written to Room immediately (local-first) with syncState=PENDING, and WorkManager schedules a background sync when connectivity is available
The note is saved to SharedPreferences as a temporary store
questions.explanation: Offline-first means writes go to the local database immediately. Room is the single source of truth. The UI reads from Room via Flow, so the change appears instantly. WorkManager is then scheduled with a NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint, so sync happens automatically when the device gets connectivity. The syncState field tracks which notes need to be pushed to the server.
hints: What is the 'single source of truth' in offline-first architecture?
What mechanism ensures sync happens even if the app is killed?

questions.title: callbackFlow for Firebase
questions.description: In the Chat app, why is callbackFlow used to wrap Firebase's addValueEventListener?
options: callbackFlow converts Firebase's callback-based API into a Kotlin Flow, allowing the ViewModel to use StateFlow and structured concurrency with lifecycle-aware collection
callbackFlow makes Firebase faster by batching updates
callbackFlow is the only way to use Firebase with Compose
callbackFlow converts synchronous code to asynchronous
questions.explanation: callbackFlow bridges callback-based APIs into the Flow world. Firebase uses ValueEventListener (a callback). By wrapping it in callbackFlow, you get a Flow<List<Message>> that the ViewModel can convert to StateFlow and the UI can collect with lifecycle awareness. The awaitClose block removes the listener when the Flow is cancelled, preventing leaks.
hints: Firebase uses old-style Java callbacks. Kotlin Flow is a reactive stream.
What does awaitClose do in callbackFlow?

questions.title: Multi-Module Dependency Rules
questions.description: In the Task Manager, why can :feature:tasklist NOT depend on :feature:taskdetail?
options: Gradle does not support dependencies between library modules
It would create a circular dependency, and it violates the principle that features should be independent and communicate only through :core or navigation
Feature modules cannot depend on anything
It would make the APK too large
questions.explanation: Feature modules must be independent to enable: (1) parallel compilation (faster builds), (2) independent development (teams can work on features without conflicts), (3) dynamic delivery (download features on demand). If features depended on each other, you would get circular dependencies and tight coupling. Features communicate indirectly — via Navigation for screen transitions, and via :core modules for shared data and UI.
hints: Think about what would happen if feature A depends on feature B and feature B depends on feature A
How do two feature screens navigate to each other without a direct dependency?

questions.title: Sealed Interface for UI State
questions.description: The Weather ViewModel uses sealed interface WeatherUiState with Initial, Loading, Success, and Error subtypes. Why use a sealed interface instead of a single data class with nullable fields?
options: Sealed interfaces are faster at runtime

The compiler enforces exhaustive when() expressions — you cannot forget to handle a state. Each state carries only its relevant data, avoiding impossible combinations like isLoading=true AND error!=null.
Sealed interfaces are required by Compose for recomposition
Sealed interfaces allow you to avoid using StateFlow
questions.explanation: A single data class like UiState(isLoading, data?, error?) allows impossible states: what does isLoading=true, error="Oops" mean? With a sealed interface, each state is a distinct type. when(state) in Compose forces you to handle every case, and the compiler warns if you add a new state and forget to handle it. Each subtype carries only its relevant data — Success has weather data, Error has a message.
hints: Think about invalid state combinations in a flat data class
What does the Kotlin compiler do with when() on a sealed type?

questions.title: LazyColumn Key Parameter
questions.description: In the Chat and Notes apps, items(key = { it.id }) is used in LazyColumn. What does the key parameter do?
options: It sorts the list by that field
It tells Compose which items are the same across recompositions, enabling correct animations, stable scroll position, and efficient diffing when the list changes
It is required by Room to identify database rows
It encrypts the items for security
questions.explanation: Without stable keys, LazyColumn uses the item's position as identity. If you insert an item at position 0, every item 'changes' position and Compose recomposes all of them. With key = { it.id }, Compose knows that item "abc" is the same item even if it moves from position 3 to position 4. This enables: efficient recomposition (only changed items recompose), correct item animations, and preserved state (e.g., text field content inside items).
hints: What happens when an item is inserted at the top of a list without keys?
Think about how Compose 'identifies' the same item across recompositions

questions.title: flatMapLatest for Search
questions.description: In the Task Manager's TaskListViewModel, flatMapLatest is used with the search query flow. Why flatMapLatest instead of flatMapConcat or flatMapMerge?
questions.answer: flatMapLatest cancels the previous inner Flow whenever a new value arrives from the upstream Flow. When the user types a new character in the search bar, the previous search query's database observation is cancelled and a new one starts for the updated query. flatMapConcat would wait for each query to complete before starting the next (wasteful for search). flatMapMerge would keep all queries active simultaneously (confusing results). flatMapLatest ensures only the most recent search is active.
questions.explanation: This is a common pattern for search-as-you-type. Each keystroke emits a new query value. flatMapLatest cancels the previous repository.searchTasks(oldQuery) Flow and starts repository.searchTasks(newQuery). This prevents stale results from appearing and avoids wasted database queries for intermediate search terms.
hints: What happens to the previous search when the user types another character?
Compare: Latest cancels previous, Concat waits, Merge keeps all

questions.title: Hilt in Multi-Module
questions.description: The Task Manager has Hilt modules in both :core:data (DataModule providing Room) and :app. How does Hilt discover the @Module in the library module?
options: You must manually register each module in the Application class

Hilt uses KSP to generate code at compile time in each module. At app assembly, the Hilt Gradle plugin merges all generated components. @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) tells Hilt which component the module belongs to.
Library modules cannot provide Hilt modules — you must move everything to :app
Hilt uses runtime reflection to scan the classpath for @Module annotations
questions.explanation: Hilt's multi-module support works at compile time, not runtime. Each module with KSP generates its own Hilt metadata. When the :app module is compiled, Hilt's KSP processor aggregates metadata from all dependencies and generates the final SingletonComponent with all providers. @InstallIn tells Hilt the scope. This is why each module that uses Hilt annotations must apply the KSP plugin.
hints: Think about when Hilt processes annotations — at compile time or runtime?
